paragraphs.en: Chris Lattner is the creator of LLVM and led the development of the Swift language at Apple. With Mojo, he's taking another big swing: How do you make the process of getting the full power out of modern GPUs productive and fun? In this episode, Ron and Chris discuss how to design a language that's easy to use while still providing the level of control required to write state of the art kernels. A key idea is to ask programmers to fully reckon with the details of the hardware, but making that work manageable and shareable via a form of type-safe metaprogramming. The aim is to support both specialization to the computation in question as well as to the hardware platform. "Somebody has to do this work," Chris says, "if we ever want to get to an ecosystem where one vendor doesn't control everything."
paragraphs.zh: Chris Lattner 是 LLVM 的创造者，也曾在苹果公司主导 Swift 语言的开发。如今他用 Mojo 再次大干一场：怎样才能让充分发挥现代 GPU 性能的过程变得高效又有趣？本期节目中，Ron 和 Chris 探讨了如何设计一门既易于使用、又能提供编写顶尖核函数所需的底层控制能力的语言。其中一个关键思路是：要求程序员直面硬件细节，但通过一种类型安全的元编程方式让这项工作变得可管理、可复用。目标是同时支持针对特定计算任务和特定硬件平台的专门化。"总要有人做这件事，"Chris 说，"如果我们希望有朝一日打造一个没有单一厂商垄断一切的生态系统的话。"

paragraphs.en: Chris Lattner is the creator of LLVM and led the development of the Swift language at Apple. With Mojo, he's taking another big swing: How do you make the process of getting the full power out of modern GPUs productive and fun? In this episode, Ron and Chris discuss how to design a language that's easy to use while still providing the level of control required to write state of the art kernels. A key idea is to ask programmers to fully reckon with the details of the hardware, but making that work manageable and shareable via a form of type-safe metaprogramming. The aim is to support both specialization to the computation in question as well as to the hardware platform. "Somebody has to do this work," Chris says, "if we ever want to get to an ecosystem where one vendor doesn't control everything."
paragraphs.zh: Chris Lattner 是 LLVM 的创造者，也曾在苹果公司主导 Swift 语言的开发。如今他用 Mojo 再次大干一场：怎样才能让充分发挥现代 GPU 性能的过程变得高效又有趣？本期节目中，Ron 和 Chris 探讨了如何设计一门既易于使用、又能提供编写顶尖核函数所需的底层控制能力的语言。其中一个关键思路是：要求程序员直面硬件细节，但通过一种类型安全的元编程方式让这项工作变得可管理、可复用。目标是同时支持针对特定计算任务和特定硬件平台的专门化。"总要有人做这件事，"Chris 说，"如果我们希望有朝一日打造一个没有单一厂商垄断一切的生态系统的话。"

paragraphs.en: Some links to topics that came up in the discussion:
paragraphs.zh: 以下是讨论中提到的一些相关链接：

paragraphs.en: Welcome to Signals and Threads, in-depth conversations about every layer of the tech stack, from Jane Street. I'm Ron Minsky. It is my great pleasure to have Chris Lattner on the show. Typically on Signals and Threads, we end up talking to engineers who work here at Jane Street, but sometimes we like to grab outside folk, and Chris is an amazing figure to bring on because he's been so involved in a bunch of really foundational pieces of computing that we all use—LLVM, and Clang, and MLIR, and OpenCL, and Swift, and now Mojo. And this has happened at a bunch of different storied institutions—Apple, and Tesla, and Google, and SiFive, and now Modular. So anyway, it's a pleasure to have you joining us, Chris.
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎收听 Signals and Threads，这是由 Jane Street 出品的一档深度对话节目，聊遍技术栈的每一层。我是 Ron Minsky。今天非常荣幸请到了 Chris Lattner。Signals and Threads 通常邀请我们 Jane Street 的内部工程师，但偶尔我们也会请来外部嘉宾。Chris 是一位了不起的人物，因为他参与创造了许多我们每天都在使用的基础计算工具——LLVM、Clang、MLIR、OpenCL、Swift，以及现在的 Mojo。这些工作发生在一系列传奇机构——苹果、特斯拉、谷歌、SiFive，以及现在的 Modular。总之，很高兴你能加入我们，Chris。

paragraphs.en: Thank you, Ron. I'm so happy to be here.
paragraphs.zh: 谢谢你，Ron。很高兴来到这里。

paragraphs.en: I guess I want to start by just hearing a little bit more about your origin story. How did you get into computing and how did you get into this world of both compiler engineering and programming language design?
paragraphs.zh: 我想先从你的成长经历聊起。你是怎么进入计算机这个领域的，又是怎么踏入编译器工程和编程语言设计这个世界的？

paragraphs.en: So I grew up in the '80s and back before computers were really a thing. We had PCs, but they weren't considered cool. And so I fell in love with understanding how the computer worked. And back then, things were way simpler. I started with a BASIC interpreter, for example, and you'd get a book from the store. Remember when we had books? [laughs] And you'd learn things from books?
paragraphs.zh: 我是在八十年代长大的，那会儿电脑还不算真正流行。我们有个人电脑，但它们不被认为是什么酷东西。所以我就爱上了搞清楚电脑是怎么工作的。那时候事情简单多了。我从 BASIC 解释器入手，然后去书店买书——还记得我们有书的年代吗？[笑] 那时候你就是从书上学东西的。

paragraphs.en: Did you do the thing where you'd get the hobbyist magazine and copy out the listing of the program?
paragraphs.zh: 你有没有做过那种事——买一本爱好者杂志，然后把程序清单一行行抄下来？

paragraphs.en: That's exactly right. And so we didn't have vibe coding, but we did have books. And so just by typing things in, you could understand how things work, and then when you broke it—because inevitably you're typing something in and you don't really know what you're doing—you have to figure out what went wrong and so it encouraged a certain amount of debugging. I really love computer games. Again, back then, things were a little bit simpler. Computer games drove graphics and performance and things like this. And so I spent some time on these things called bulletin board systems and the early internet reading about how game programmers are trying to push the limits of the hardware. And so that's where I got interested in performance and computers and systems. I went on to college and had an amazing professor at my school, shout out to University of Portland in Portland, Oregon, and he was a compiler nerd.
paragraphs.zh: 就是这样。那时候没有什么「氛围编程」，但我们有书。光靠一个字一个字地敲进去，你就能理解事情是怎么运作的。然后你把它搞坏了——因为你总会敲进一些自己都不知道在干嘛的东西——你就得搞清楚哪里出了问题，这就逼着你去调试。我真的很喜欢电脑游戏。那个年代事情还算简单，电脑游戏驱动着图形和性能的发展。我在那些叫做电子公告板系统的地方和早期互联网上读了大量内容，了解游戏程序员怎么把硬件逼到极限。就这样，我开始对性能、计算机和系统产生了兴趣。后来我上了大学，在学校遇到了一位很棒的教授——在此向俄勒冈州波特兰的波特兰大学致敬——他是个编译器狂热爱好者。

paragraphs.en: And so, I think that his love for compilers was infectious. His name was Steven Vegdahl, and that caused me to go on to pursue compilers at University of Illinois. And there again, continue to fall down this rabbit hole of compilers and systems, and build LLVM. And ever since I got into the compiler world, I loved it. I love compilers because they're large-scale systems, there's multiple different components that all work together. And in the university setting, it was really cool in the compiler class, because unlike most of the assignments where you do an assignment, turn it in, forget about it—in compilers, you would do an assignment, turn it in, get graded, and then build on it. And it felt much more realistic like software engineering, rather than just doing a project to get graded.
paragraphs.zh: 他对编译器的热爱很有感染力。他叫 Steven Vegdahl，正是他让我决定去伊利诺伊大学继续钻研编译器。在那里，我继续深陷编译器和系统这个兔子洞，并且构建了 LLVM。自从踏入编译器的世界，我就爱上了它。我喜欢编译器，因为它是大型系统，由多个不同的组件协同工作。在大学的编译器课上，有一点特别棒：和大多数作业不同——做完交上去就忘了——编译器的作业是做完交上去、拿到成绩，然后在上面继续构建。这感觉更像真实的软件工程，而不只是为了拿分而做个项目。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, I think for a lot of people, the OS class is their first real experience of doing a thing where you really are building layer on top of layer. I think it's an incredibly important experience for people as they start engineering.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，我觉得对很多人来说，操作系统课是他们第一次真正体验到层层叠加地构建一个东西。我认为这对于刚开始做工程的人来说是一段非常重要的经历。

paragraphs.en: It's also one where you get to use some of those data structures. I took this, almost academic, here's what a binary tree is, and here's what a graph is. And particularly when I went through it, it was taught from a very math-forward perspective, but it really made it useful. And so that was actually really cool. I'm like, 'Oh, this is why I learned this stuff.'
paragraphs.zh: 而且那门课也让你真正用上了那些数据结构。学二叉树是什么、图是什么，这些都带着浓厚的学术气息。尤其是我那时候，课程是从很偏数学的角度来讲的，但它让这些东西真正派上了用场。这其实很酷，我当时心想，「哦，原来这就是为什么要学这些东西。」

paragraphs.en: So one thing that strikes me about your career is that you've ended up going back and forth between compiler engineering and language design space, whereas I feel like a lot of people are on one side or the other—they're mostly compilers people and they don't care that much about the language, and just, how do we make this thing go fast? And there are some people who are really focusing on language design and the work on the compiler is a secondary thing towards that design. And you've both popped back and forth. And then also a lot of your compiler engineering work, really starting with LLVM, in some sense is itself, very language-forward. With LLVM, there's a language in there that's this intermediate language that you're surfacing as a tool for people to use. So I'm just curious to hear more about how you think about the back and forth between compiler engineering and language design.
paragraphs.zh: 你职业生涯中有一点让我很感触，就是你在编译器工程和语言设计之间来回穿梭。在我看来，很多人要么偏向其中一边——他们主要是编译器人，对语言本身不太在意，只关心怎么让这东西跑得更快；要么专注于语言设计，而编译器工作只是为设计服务的次要手段。但你却两边都做，来回切换。另外，你很多编译器工程工作，从 LLVM 开始，从某种意义上说本身就非常重视语言层面。LLVM 里有一门中间语言，你把它作为工具开放给大家使用。所以我很好奇，你是怎么看待编译器工程和语言设计之间这种来回切换的？

paragraphs.en: The reason I do this is that effectively, my career is following my own interests. And so my interests are not static. I want to work on different kinds of problems and solve useful problems and build into things. And so the more technology and capability you have, the higher you can reach. And so with LLVM, for example, built and learned a whole bunch of cool stuff about deep code generation for an X86 chip and that category of technology with register allocation, stuff like this. But then it made it possible to go, say, let's go tackle C++ and let's go use this to build the world's best implementation of something that lots more people use and understand than deep backend code generation technology. And then with Swift, it was, build even higher and say, 'Okay, well C++, maybe some people like it, but I think we can do better and let's reach higher.' I've also been involved in AI systems, been involved in building an iPad app to help teach kids how to code. And so, lots of different things over time. And so for me, the place I think I'm most useful and where a lot of my experience is valuable ends up being at this hardware-software boundary.
paragraphs.zh: 我这么做的原因，说白了就是我的职业生涯在跟着自己的兴趣走。我的兴趣不是一成不变的。我想做各种不同类型的问题，解决有用的问题，不断构建新东西。技术和能力积累越多，你就能站得越高。比如做 LLVM，在 X86 芯片的深度代码生成、寄存器分配这类技术上学到了一大堆很酷的东西。然后这就让我有可能去做 C++，去用这些技术构建世界上最好的 C++ 实现——这是比深度后端代码生成更多人使用和理解的东西。然后到了 Swift，就是站得更高，说，C++ 也许有人喜欢，但我觉得我们可以做得更好，再往上走一步。我也参与过 AI 系统，也参与过构建一个帮助孩子学编程的 iPad 应用。总之，这些年做了很多不同的事情。对我来说，我觉得自己最有价值、经验最能发挥作用的地方，最终还是在硬件与软件的交界处。

paragraphs.en: I'm curious how you ended up making the leap to working on Swift. From my perspective, Swift looks from the outside, like one of these points of arrival in mainstream programming contexts of a bunch of ideas that I have long thought are really great ideas in other programming languages. And I'm curious, in some ways a step away from like, oh, I'm going to work on really low-level stuff and compiler optimization, and then we will go much higher level and do a C++ implementation, which is still a pretty low level. How did the whole Swift thing happen?
paragraphs.zh: 我很好奇你是怎么跳到 Swift 上去的。从我的角度来看，Swift 看起来就像是在主流编程领域中，那些我长期以来认为在其他编程语言里非常棒的想法终于汇聚到一起的一个成果。而且在某种程度上，这是个跨度很大的跳跃——从做底层的东西和编译器优化，到做 C++ 实现（这已经算是相对高层了），再到 Swift。这整件事是怎么发生的？

paragraphs.en: Great question. I mean, the timeframe for people that aren't familiar is that LLVM started in 2000. So by 2005, I had exited university and I joined Apple. And so LLVM was an advanced research project at that point. By the 2010 timeframe, LLVM was much more mature and we had just shipped C++ support in Clang, and so it could bootstrap itself, which means the compiler could compile itself. It's all written in C++, it could build advanced libraries like the Boost template library, which is super crazy advanced template stuff. And so the C++ implementation that I and the team had built was real. Now, C++ in my opinion, is not a beautiful programming language. And so implementing it is a very interesting technical challenge. For me, a lot of problem-solving ends up being, how do you factor the system the right way?
paragraphs.zh: 好问题。对不熟悉这段历史的人来说，时间线是这样的：LLVM 始于 2000 年。到 2005 年，我离开了大学，加入了苹果。那时候 LLVM 还是一个前沿研究项目。到了 2010 年前后，LLVM 已经相当成熟，我们刚刚在 Clang 中完成了 C++ 支持，它可以自举了——也就是说编译器可以编译自身。整个东西用 C++ 写成，能构建像 Boost 模板库这样极度复杂的高级库。所以我和团队构建的 C++ 实现是真实可用的。不过在我看来，C++ 并不是一门优美的编程语言。所以实现它是一个很有趣的技术挑战。对我来说，很多问题的解决最终归结为：怎么把系统拆解得合理？

paragraphs.en: And so Clang has some really cool stuff that allowed it to scale and things like that, but I was also burned out. We had just shipped it. It was amazing. I'm like, there has to be something better. And so, Swift really came starting in 2010. It was a nights and weekends project. It wasn't like top-down management said, 'Let's go build a new programming language.' It was 'Chris being burned out'—I was running a 20 to 40 person team at the time, being an engineer during the day, and being a technical leader, but then needing an escape hatch. And so I said, 'Okay, well, I think we can have something better. I have a lot of good ideas. Turns out, programming languages are a mature space. It's not like you need to invent pattern matching at this point. It's embarrassing that C++ doesn't have good pattern matching.
paragraphs.zh: Clang 里有很多很酷的东西让它得以规模化，但我也精疲力竭了。我们刚刚发布了它，成果很了不起。我心想，一定有更好的东西。所以 Swift 就是从 2010 年开始的，那是一个利用夜晚和周末做的项目。不是什么自上而下的管理层说「我们去构建一门新编程语言」，而是「Chris 精疲力竭了」——我当时带着一个 20 到 40 人的团队，白天是工程师，也是技术负责人，但需要一个出口。于是我对自己说，好，我觉得我们可以做出更好的东西，我有很多好想法。结果发现，编程语言是一个成熟的领域，不是说在这个时间点上你还需要发明模式匹配之类的东西。C++ 没有好的模式匹配，这实在令人汗颜。

paragraphs.en: We should just pause for a second, because I think this is like a small but really essential thing. I think the single best feature coming out of language like ML in the mid-seventies is, first of all, this notion of an algebraic data type, meaning every programming language on earth has a way of saying this and that and the other, a record, or a class, or a tuple.
paragraphs.zh: 我们先停一下，因为我觉得这是个小却非常关键的点。我认为 ML 这类语言在七十年代中期带来的最好的特性，首先就是代数数据类型这个概念——地球上每种编程语言都有表达「这个、那个和另一个」的方式，比如记录、类或者元组。

paragraphs.en: A weird programming language, I think it was Barbara Liskov?
paragraphs.zh: 还有一门奇特的编程语言，我记得是 Barbara Liskov 吧？

paragraphs.en: Yeah. And she did a lot of the early theorizing about, 'What are abstract data types?' But the ability to do this or that or the other, to have data types that are a union of different possible shapes of the data—and then having this pattern matching facility that lets you basically in a reliable way do the case analysis so you can break down what the possibilities are—is just incredibly useful. And very few mainstream languages have picked it up. I mean Swift again is an example, but languages like ML, SML, and Haskell, and OCaml—
paragraphs.zh: 对，她在早期做了大量关于「什么是抽象数据类型」的理论工作。但是拥有那种能表达「这个或那个或另一个」的能力，也就是数据类型可以是不同数据形态的联合，然后再有模式匹配这种机制，让你能可靠地做情况分析、拆解所有可能性——这实在是非常有用。然而很少有主流语言采纳了这个东西。Swift 算一个例子，但像 ML、SML、Haskell 和 OCaml 这些语言——

paragraphs.en: Standard!
paragraphs.zh: Standard！

paragraphs.en: That's right. SML. Standard ML. It's been there for a long time.
paragraphs.zh: 对，SML，Standard ML，这已经存在很久了。

paragraphs.en: I mean pattern matching, it is not an exotic feature. Here we're talking about 2010. C# didn't have it. C++ didn't have it. Obviously Java didn't have it. I don't think JavaScript had it. None of these mainstream languages had it, but it's obvious. And so part of my opinion about that—and so by the way, I represent as engineer, I'm not actually a mathematician, and so type theory goes way over my head. I don't really understand this. The thing that gets me frustrated about the academic approach to programming languages is that people approach it by saying there's sum types, and there's intersection types, and there's these types, and they don't start from utility forward. And so pattern matching, when I learned OCaml, it's so beautiful. It makes it so easy and expressive to build very simple things. And so to me, I always identify to the utility and then yes, there's amazing formal type theory behind it, and that's great and that's why it actually works and composes. But bringing that stuff forward and focusing on utility and the problems it solves, and how it makes people happy, ends up being the thing that I think moves the needle in terms of adoption, at least in mainstream.
paragraphs.zh: 模式匹配根本不是什么稀奇古怪的特性。我们说的是 2010 年。C# 没有，C++ 没有，Java 显然也没有，JavaScript 应该也没有。这些主流语言没有一个有，但这个东西明明很显然应该有。这也是我观点的一部分——顺便说一下，我代表的是工程师的立场，我不是数学家，类型理论远超出了我的理解范围。学术界对编程语言的那种研究方式让我有点抓狂，因为他们开口就是和类型、交叉类型、这种类型那种类型，而不是从实用性出发。而模式匹配，当我学 OCaml 的时候，美极了。它让构建简单的东西变得那么容易、那么有表达力。所以对我来说，我总是先认同它的实用价值，然后确实，它背后有很了不起的形式类型理论，那很好，这也是它真正有效且能组合的原因。但把那些东西推到前面、聚焦于实用性、它解决的问题以及它如何让人开心，才是我认为真正能推动采用率的事情，至少在主流领域是这样。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, I mean I think that's right. My approach also, and my interest in language is also very much not from the mathematical perspective, although my undergraduate degree is in math. I like math a lot, but I mostly approach these things as a practitioner. But the thing I've been struck by over the years is the value of having these features have a really strong mathematical foundation is they generalize, and as you were saying, compose much better. If they are in the end mathematically simple, you're way more likely to have a feature that actually pans out as it gets used way beyond your initial view as to what the thing was for.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，我也这么认为。我对语言的方式和兴趣同样不是从数学角度出发的，尽管我本科学的是数学。我很喜欢数学，但我主要以实践者的身份来看待这些事情。不过这些年来让我深有感触的一点是：让这些特性拥有扎实数学基础的价值，在于它们更好地泛化，正如你所说，也能更好地组合。如果在根本上是数学上简洁的，你就更有可能得到一个在被使用到远超你最初设想的场景时依然成立的特性。

paragraphs.en: That's right. This is actually a personal defect because I don't understand the math in the way that maybe theoretically would be ideal. I end up having to rediscover certain truths that are obvious. The cliche, 'If the Russian mathematician invented it 50 years ago…' And so a lot of what I find is that I can find truth and beauty when things compose and things fit together, and often I'll find out it's already been discovered because everything in programming language has been done. There's almost nothing novel, but still that design process of saying, let's pull things together, let's reason about why it doesn't quite fit together. Let's go figure out how to better factor this. Let's figure out how to make it simpler these days. That process to me, I think is kind of like people working on physics, [from what] I hear. The simpler the outcome becomes, the more close to truth it feels like it is. And so I share that—and maybe it's more design gene or engineer-design combination, but it's probably what you mathematicians actually know inherently, and I just haven't figured it out yet.
paragraphs.zh: 没错，这其实是我个人的一个缺陷，因为我对数学的理解方式可能并不理想。我最终往往要自己重新发现一些显而易见的道理。那句老话，「如果俄罗斯数学家五十年前就发明了这个……」。所以我发现，当事物能够组合在一起、各部分契合时，我就能感受到真理和美感，而且经常会发现这其实早就被人发现了，因为编程语言领域几乎所有事情都被做过了，几乎没有什么是真正新颖的。但是那个设计过程——让我们把东西拼在一起，推理为什么它们不太合拍，想办法更好地拆解它，想办法让它更简洁——这个过程对我来说，有点像听说的那些做物理的人。结果越简洁，感觉就越接近真理。我也有同感——也许这更多是一种设计基因或工程加设计的结合，但这大概是你们数学家骨子里就知道的东西，而我还没想明白。

paragraphs.en: Do you find yourself doing things after you come to it from an engineering perspective, trying to figure out whether there are useful mathematical insights? Do you go back and read the papers? Do you have other PL people who are more mathematically oriented who you talk to? How do you extend your thinking to cover some of that other stuff?
paragraphs.zh: 你从工程角度得出一些想法之后，会不会反过去试图找找是否有有用的数学洞见？你会回去读论文吗？你有没有一些数学背景更强的编程语言领域的人可以交流？你是怎么扩展自己的思路去覆盖那些部分的？

paragraphs.en: See, the problem is math is scary to me. So I see Greek letters and I run away. I do follow arXiv and things like this, and there's a programming language section on that. And so I get into some of it, but what I get attracted to in that is the examples and the results section and the future-looking parts of it. And so it's not necessarily the 'how,' it's the 'what it means.' And so I think a lot of that really speaks to me. The other thing that really speaks to me when you talk about language design and things like this is blog posts from some obscure academic programming language that I've never heard of. You just have somebody talking about algebraic effect systems for this and that and the other thing, or something really fancy, but they figure out how to explain it in a way that's useful. And so when it's not just, 'Let me explain to you the type system,' but it's, 'Let me explain this problem this fancy feature enables,' that's where I get excited. That's where it speaks to me because, again, I'm problem-oriented, and having a beautiful way to express and solve problems, I appreciate.
paragraphs.zh: 问题是数学让我害怕。一看到希腊字母我就想逃。我确实关注 arXiv 之类的东西，上面有编程语言的板块。所以我会看一些，但吸引我的是里面的例子、结果部分和展望未来的那些内容。所以不一定是「怎么做」，而是「这意味着什么」。我觉得这些东西很能打动我。谈到语言设计之类的，另一个很能打动我的，是那些我从没听说过的偏门学术编程语言的博客文章。就是有人在谈代数效果系统之类的东西，或者什么很高深的概念，但他们想出了一种有用的方式来解释它。所以当它不只是「让我来解释这个类型系统」，而是「让我来解释这个高级特性解决了什么问题」，我就会兴奋起来。这能打动我，因为我是问题导向的，能以美妙的方式表达和解决问题，我非常欣赏。

paragraphs.en: I think there's a lot of value in the work that's done in papers of really working out in detail the theory and the math and how it all fits together. [And] I think the fact that the world has been filled with a lot of interesting blog posts from the same people has been great because I think it's another modality where it often encourages you to pull out the simpler and easier-to-consume versions of those ideas. And I think that is just a different kind of insight and it's valuable to surface that too.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得论文中把理论和数学以及它们如何组合在一起详细论证出来的那些工作，有很大的价值。而这个世界上同时也充满了来自同一批人写的大量有趣的博客文章，我觉得这很好，因为这是另一种形式，往往能鼓励你把那些想法提炼成更简单、更易消化的版本。我认为这是一种不同的洞见，把它呈现出来也是有价值的。

paragraphs.en: And also when I look at those blog posts, sometimes they design smell. Particularly the C++ community, there's a lot of really good work to fix C++. They're adding a lot of stuff to it, and C++ will never get simpler—you can't really remove things, right? And so a lot of the challenge there is, it's constrained problem-solving. And so when I look at that, often what I'll see when I'm reading one of those posts, and again, these are brilliant people and they're doing God's work trying to solve problems with C++, best of luck with that. But you look at that and you realize there's a grain of sand in the system that didn't need to be there. And so to me, it's like if you remove that grain of sand, then the entire system gets relaxed and suddenly all these constraints fall away and you can get to something much simpler. Swift, for example, it's a wonderful language and it's grown really well and the community is amazing, but it has a few grains of sand in it that cause it to be a lot more complicated. And so this is where I'm not just happy with things that got built. LLVM is amazing, it's very practical, but it has lots of problems. That's why when I get a chance to build a next generation system, I want to learn from that and actually try to solve these problems.
paragraphs.zh: 另外，当我看那些博客文章的时候，有时候会闻到一些设计上的坏味道。尤其是 C++ 社区，有很多人在认真地做修复 C++ 的工作，在往里加各种东西。C++ 永远不会变得更简单——你没办法真正删掉东西，对吧？所以很多挑战本质上是在约束条件下解题。当我读这些文章的时候，我经常看到——这些人都很聪明，他们在尽力解决 C++ 的问题，祝他们好运。但你看着看着就意识到，系统里有一粒沙子本来不该存在。对我来说，这就好像，如果你把那粒沙子移走，整个系统就松动了，所有的约束突然消失了，你就能达到简单得多的结果。比如 Swift，它是一门很棒的语言，发展得很好，社区也很了不起，但它里面有几粒沙子，让它变得复杂得多。所以这也是我不满足于已有成果的原因。LLVM 很厉害，非常实用，但它也有很多问题。这就是为什么当我有机会构建下一代系统时，我想从中学习，真正去解决这些问题。

paragraphs.en: So this is the great privilege of getting to work on a new language, which is a thing you're doing now. There's this new language called Mojo, and it's being done by this company that you co-founded called Modular. Maybe just so we understand the context a little bit, can you tell me a little bit about, what is Modular? What's the basic offering? What's the business model?
paragraphs.zh: 这就是做一门新语言的绝大特权，而这正是你现在正在做的事情。有一门叫做 Mojo 的新语言，是由你联合创办的 Modular 公司做的。为了让我们了解一些背景，能不能简单介绍一下，Modular 是什么？基本的产品是什么？商业模式是什么？

paragraphs.en: Before I even get there, I'll share more of how I got here. If you oversimplify my background, I did this LLVM thing and its foundational compiler technology for CPUs. It helped unite a lot of CPU-era infrastructure and it provided a platform for languages like Swift, but also Rust, and Julia, and many different systems that all got built on top of, and I think it really catalyzed and enabled a lot of really cool applications of accelerated compiler technology. People use LLVM in databases and for query engine optimization, lots of cool stuff. Maybe you use it for trading or something. I mean, there can be tons of different applications for this kind of technology—and then [I] did programming language stuff with Swift. But in the meantime, AI happened. And so with AI brought this entirely new generation of compute: GPUs, tensor processing units, large-scale AI training systems, FPGAs, and ASICs and all this complexity for compute, and LLVM never really worked in that system.
paragraphs.zh: 在说这个之前，我想先多分享一点我是怎么走到这里的。如果把我的经历简单概括，我做了 LLVM，那是面向 CPU 的基础编译器技术。它帮助统一了大量 CPU 时代的基础设施，为 Swift、Rust、Julia 等很多不同的系统提供了平台，这些系统都是在上面构建的。我认为它真正催化并支撑了很多很酷的加速编译器技术应用。人们把 LLVM 用在数据库和查询引擎优化里，还有很多很酷的东西。也许你们用它来做交易或者其他什么。这类技术有数不清的应用场景——然后我做了 Swift 这边的编程语言工作。但与此同时，AI 发生了。AI 带来了全新一代的计算：GPU、张量处理单元、大规模 AI 训练系统、FPGA、ASIC，计算的复杂性爆炸式增长，而 LLVM 在这套系统里从来没有真正发挥作用。

paragraphs.en: And so one of the things that I built when I was at Google was a bunch of foundational compiler technology for that category of systems. And there's this compiler technology called MLIR. MLIR is basically LLVM 2.0. And so take everything you learn from building LLVM and helping solve this, but then bring it forward into this next generation of compiler technology so that you can go hopefully unify the world's compute for this GPU and AI and ASIC kind of world. MLIR has been amazingly successful, and I think it's used in roughly every one of these AI systems and GPUs. It's used by Nvidia, it's used by Google, it's used by roughly everybody in this space. But one of the challenges is that there hasn't been unification. And so you have these very large-scale AI software platforms. You have CUDA from Nvidia, you have XLA from Google, you have ROCm from AMD.
paragraphs.zh: 我在谷歌的时候，为那一类系统构建了一批基础编译器技术。这套编译器技术叫做 MLIR。MLIR 基本上就是 LLVM 2.0。把构建 LLVM 时学到的一切带着向前走，进入下一代编译器技术，希望能统一 GPU、AI 和 ASIC 这个世界的计算。MLIR 取得了惊人的成功，我认为它几乎被每一个 AI 系统和 GPU 厂商使用。Nvidia 在用，谷歌在用，这个领域几乎所有人都在用。但挑战在于，这种统一并没有实现。现在有很多大型 AI 软件平台：Nvidia 的 CUDA，谷歌的 XLA，AMD 的 ROCm。

paragraphs.en: It's countless. Every company has their own software stack. And one of the things that I discovered and encountered, and I think the entire world sees, is that there's this incredible fragmentation driven by the fact that each of these software stacks built by a hardware maker are just all completely different. And some of them work better than others, but regardless, it's a gigantic mess. And there's these really cool high-level technologies like PyTorch that we all love and we want to use. But if PyTorch is built on completely different stacks and schooling together these megalithic worlds from different vendors, it's very difficult to get something that works.
paragraphs.zh: 多得数不清，每家公司都有自己的软件栈。我发现并亲身遭遇的，也是整个世界都看到的一个现象，就是这种令人难以置信的碎片化，根源在于每家硬件厂商构建的软件栈都完全不同。有些比其他的更好用，但不管怎样，这是个巨大的烂摊子。而且我们都喜欢也想用 PyTorch 这样很酷的高层技术。但如果 PyTorch 是建立在完全不同的技术栈上，硬要把来自不同厂商的这些庞然大物捏合在一起，要让它真正运转起来非常困难。

paragraphs.en: Right. They're both complicated trade-offs around the performance that you get out of different tools and then also a different set of complicated trade-offs around how hard they are to use, how complicated it is to write something in them, and then what hardware you can target from each individual one. And each of these ecosystems is churning just incredibly fast. There's always new hardware coming out and new vendors in new places, and there's also new little languages popping up into existence, and it makes the whole thing pretty hard to wrangle.
paragraphs.zh: 对，这里面有两套复杂的权衡：一套是关于从不同工具能得到多少性能，另一套是关于它们有多难用、用它们写东西有多复杂，以及每种工具分别能跑在哪些硬件上。而且这些生态系统都在以惊人的速度翻腾变化。总有新硬件出来，总有新厂商出现在新的地方，还有新的小语言不断涌现，这让整件事变得很难驾驭。

paragraphs.en: Exactly. And AI is moving so fast. There's a new model every week. It's crazy. And new applications, new research, the amount of money being dumped into this by everybody is just incredible. And so how does anybody keep up? It's a structural problem in the industry. And so the structural problem is that the people doing this kind of work, the people doing code generation for advanced GPUs and things like this, they're all at hardware companies. And the hardware companies, every single one of them is building their own stack because they have to. There is nothing to plug into. There's nothing like 'LLVM but for AI,' that doesn't exist. And so as they go and build their own vertical software stack, of course they're focused on their hardware, they got advanced roadmaps, they have a new chip coming out next year, they're plowing their energy and time into solving for their hardware. But we, out in the industry, we actually want something else. We want to be able to have software that runs across multiple pieces of hardware. And so, if everybody doing the work is at a hardware company, it's very natural that you get this fragmentation across vendors because nobody's incentivized to go work together. And even if they're incentivized, they don't have time to go work on somebody else's chip. AMD is not going to pay to work on Nvidia GPUs or something like this.
paragraphs.zh: 没错，而且 AI 发展太快了。每周都有新模型出来，太疯狂了。新应用、新研究，所有人往里砸的钱多得令人咋舌。那么，谁能跟得上？这是行业的结构性问题。结构性问题在于，做这类工作的人——为先进 GPU 做代码生成之类的人——都在硬件公司里。硬件公司，每一家都在构建自己的软件栈，因为他们不得不这样做。没有什么现成的东西可以对接。不存在所谓「AI 版的 LLVM」，那东西根本不存在。所以他们各自构建自己的垂直软件栈，当然是聚焦在自家硬件上，有着先进的路线图，明年又有新芯片要出，把精力和时间都投入到解决自家硬件的问题上。但我们，在业界的人，实际上想要别的东西。我们想要能跨多种硬件运行的软件。所以，如果所有做这类工作的人都在硬件公司里，这种跨厂商的碎片化就是很自然的结果，因为没有人有动力去协同合作。就算有动力，也没有时间去做别人芯片上的工作。AMD 不会花钱去做 Nvidia GPU 上的工作，诸如此类。

paragraphs.en: That's true when you think about this, kind of, a split between low-level and high-level languages. So Nvidia has CUDA and AMD has ROCm, which is mostly a clone of CUDA, and then the XLA tools from Google work incredibly well on TPUs, and so on and so forth. Different vendors have different things. Then there's the high-level tools, PyTorch, and JAX, and Triton, and various things like that. And those are typically actually not made by the hardware vendors. Those are made by different kinds of users—I guess Google is responsible for some of these and they're also sometimes a hardware vendor—but a lot of the time it's more stepped back. Although even there, the cross-platform support is complicated and messy and incomplete.
paragraphs.zh: 这一点当你想到底层语言和高层语言之间的分裂时尤为明显。Nvidia 有 CUDA，AMD 有 ROCm（基本是 CUDA 的克隆），谷歌的 XLA 工具在 TPU 上表现极好，如此等等。不同厂商有不同的东西。然后还有高层工具，PyTorch、JAX、Triton 之类的。这些通常不是硬件厂商做的，而是由不同类型的用户做的——谷歌对其中一些负责，但他们有时也是硬件厂商——但很多时候是更超脱一些的人在做。即便如此，跨平台支持依然复杂、混乱、不完整。

paragraphs.en: Because they're built on top of fundamentally incompatible things. And so that's the fundamental nature. And so again, you go back to Chris's dysfunction and my weird career choices, I always end up back at the hardware-software boundary, and there's a lot of other folks that are really good at adding very high-level abstractions. If you go back a few years ago, MLOps was the cool thing, and it was, 'Let's build a layer of Python on top of TensorFlow and PyTorch and build a unified AI platform.' But the problem with that, is that building abstractions on top of two things that don't work very well, can't solve performance, or liability, or management, or these other problems. You can only add a layer of duct tape, but as soon as something goes wrong, you end up having to debug this entire crazy stack of stuff that you really didn't want to have to know about.
paragraphs.zh: 因为它们建立在根本不兼容的东西之上。这是根本性的问题。所以，又回到 Chris 的执念和我奇怪的职业选择——我总是最终回到硬件与软件的交界处。有很多人非常擅长在顶层添加高层抽象。回到几年前，MLOps 是最时髦的东西，就是「在 TensorFlow 和 PyTorch 上再加一层 Python，构建一个统一的 AI 平台」。但问题是，在两个本来就运作得不太好的东西上添加抽象，解决不了性能、可靠性、管理性或者其他这些问题。你只能再加一层胶带，但一旦出了什么问题，你就不得不调试整个疯狂的技术栈，而那些东西你本来根本不想去了解。

paragraphs.en: And so it's a leaky abstraction. And so the genesis of Modular (bringing it back to this) was realizing there are structural problems in the industry. There is nobody that's incentivized to go build a unifying software platform and do that work at the bottom level. And so what we set off to do is we said, 'Okay, let's go build…'—and there's different ways of explaining this. You could say 'a replacement for CUDA,' that's like a flamboyant way to say this, but 'let's go build a successor to all of this technology that is better than what the hardware makers are building, and is portable.' And so what this takes, is doing the work that these hardware companies are doing, and I set the goal for the team of saying, let's do it better than, for example, Nvidia is doing it for their own hardware.
paragraphs.zh: 所以这是一个漏洞百出的抽象。Modular 的起源就是这样——意识到行业里存在结构性问题，没有人有动力去构建一个统一的软件平台，在底层做那些工作。所以我们决定——有不同的说法可以解释这件事。你可以说「构建 CUDA 的替代品」，这是个比较张扬的说法，但说白了就是「去构建所有这些技术的下一代，比硬件厂商正在做的更好，而且可移植」。要做到这一点，需要做那些硬件公司在做的工作。我给团队设定的目标是：把它做得比 Nvidia 为自家硬件做的更好。

paragraphs.en: Which is no easy feat, right? They've got a lot of very strong engineers and they understand their hardware better than anyone does. Beating them on their own hardware is tough.
paragraphs.zh: 这可不是件容易的事，对吧？他们有很多非常强的工程师，而且比任何人都更了解自家硬件。在他们自己的硬件上超过他们，很难。

paragraphs.en: That is really hard. And they've got a 20-year head start, because CUDA is about 20 years old. They've got all the momentum. They're a pretty big company. As you say, lots of smart people. And so that was a ridiculous goal. Why did I do that? Well, I mean a certain amount of confidence in understanding how the technology worked, having a bet on what I thought we could build and the approach, and some insight and intuition, but also realizing that it's actually destiny. Somebody has to do this work. If we ever want to get to an ecosystem where one vendor doesn't control everything, if we want to get the best out of the hardware, if we want to get new programming language technologies, if we want pattern matching on a GPU—I mean, come on, this isn't rocket science—then we need at some point to do this. And if nobody else is going to do it, I'll step up and do that. That's where Modular came from—saying, 'Let's go crack this thing open. I don't know how long it will take, but sometimes it's worthwhile doing really hard things if they're valuable to the world.' And the belief was it could be profoundly impactful and hopefully get more people into even just being able to use this new form of compute with GPUs and accelerators and all this stuff, and just really redemocratize AI compute.
paragraphs.zh: 确实非常难。他们有 20 年的先发优势，因为 CUDA 大约已经有 20 年的历史了。他们有所有的势头，是一家相当大的公司，就像你说的，有很多聪明人。所以这是个荒谬的目标。我为什么要这么做？嗯，一方面是对技术工作原理有一定的自信，对我们能做什么和用什么方法有所押注，还有一些洞察和直觉。但也意识到这是命中注定的事。总要有人来做这件事。如果我们希望有朝一日打造一个没有单一厂商控制一切的生态系统，如果我们想从硬件中榨取最大的性能，如果我们想要新的编程语言技术，如果我们想在 GPU 上有模式匹配——这又不是火箭科学——那么迟早要有人来做这件事。如果没有别人去做，我来做。Modular 就是这么来的——说，让我们去把这件事搞定。我不知道要多久，但如果一件事对世界有价值，做再难的事也值得。我相信这件事可以产生深远的影响，希望能让更多人有能力使用 GPU 和各种加速器这种新的计算形式，真正让 AI 计算再次民主化。

paragraphs.en: So you pointed out that there's a real structural problem here, and I'm actually wondering how, at a business model level, do you want to solve the structural problem? Which is, the history of computing is these days littered with the bodies of companies that try to sell a programming language. It's a really hard business. How is Modular set up so that it's incented to build this platform in a way that can be a shared platform that isn't subject to just one other vendor's lock-in?
paragraphs.zh: 你指出了一个真实的结构性问题，我实际上想知道，在商业模式层面，你打算怎么解决这个结构性问题？计算机发展史上，那些试图靠卖一门编程语言维生的公司，如今已经尸横遍野。这是个非常难做的生意。Modular 是怎么设置激励机制的，让它有动力去构建这个平台，并以一种共享的方式运营，而不是落入另一个厂商的锁定？

paragraphs.en: First answer is, don't sell a programming language. As you say, that's very difficult. So we're not doing that. Go take Mojo, go use it for free. We're not selling a programming language. What we're doing is we're investing in this foundational technology to unify hardware. Our view is, as we've seen in many other domains, once you fix the foundation, now you can build high-value services for enterprises. And so our enterprise layer, often what we talk to, you end up with these groups where you have hundreds or thousands of GPUs. Often it's rented from a cloud on a three-year commit. You have a platform team that's carrying pagers and they need to keep all this stuff running and all the production workloads running. And then you have these product teams that are inventing new stuff all the time, and there's new research, there's a new model that comes out and they want to get it on the production infrastructure, but none of this stuff actually works.
paragraphs.zh: 第一个答案是，不要卖编程语言。正如你说的，那很难。所以我们不这么做。去拿 Mojo，免费用。我们不卖编程语言。我们在做的是投资于这套统一硬件的基础技术。我们的看法是，正如我们在很多其他领域看到的，一旦你把基础搞定，就可以在上面为企业构建高价值的服务。我们的企业层面的客户，通常是这样的团队：他们有几百甚至几千张 GPU，通常是从云服务商那里租的，签了三年的合同。有一个平台团队随时待命，需要让所有这些东西和所有生产负载保持运转。然后还有产品团队，不停地在搞新东西，有新的研究，有新的模型出来，他们想把它跑在生产基础设施上，但这些东西实际上都没法好好运行。

paragraphs.en: And so the software ecosystem we have with all these brilliant but crazy open source tools that are thrashing around, all these different versions of CUDA and libraries, all this different hardware happening, is just a gigantic mess. And so, helping solve this for the platform engineering team that actually needs to have stuff work, and want to be able to reason about it, and want good observability and manageability and scalability and things like this is actually, we think, very interesting. We've gotten a lot of good responses from people on that. The cost of doing this is we want to actually make it work, that's where we do fundamental language compiler underlying systems technology and help bring together these accelerators so that we can get, for example, the best performance on an AMD GPU and get it so that the software comes out in the same release train as support for an Nvidia GPU. And being able to pull that together, again, it just multiplicatively reduces complexity, which then leads to a product that actually works, which is really cool and very novel in AI.
paragraphs.zh: 我们现在的软件生态系统，有这些聪明但疯狂的开源工具在乱打架，各种不同版本的 CUDA 和各种库，各种不同的硬件，这就是个巨大的烂摊子。帮助平台工程团队解决这个问题——他们需要东西能正常工作，需要能推理清楚，需要良好的可观察性、可管理性和可扩展性——我们认为这是非常有意思的事情。我们从很多人那里得到了不错的反馈。做到这一点的代价是，我们需要真正让它运行起来，这就是我们做基础语言编译器底层系统技术的地方，帮助把这些加速器整合在一起，比如在 AMD GPU 上取得最佳性能，并让相应的软件和对 Nvidia GPU 的支持在同一个版本中一起发布。把这些整合在一起，又一次成倍地降低了复杂性，从而产生一个真正能用的产品，这在 AI 领域很酷，也很新鲜。

paragraphs.en: So the way that Mojo plays in here, is it basically lets you provide the best possible performance and I guess the best possible performance across multiple different hardware platforms. Are you primarily thinking about this as an inference platform, or, how does the training world fit in?
paragraphs.zh: 那么 Mojo 在这里的作用，是让你能在多种不同硬件平台上提供尽可能最好的性能。你主要是把这个当作推理平台来考虑，还是说，训练这块儿是怎么融入的？

paragraphs.en: So let me zoom in and I'll explain our technology components. I have a blog post series I encourage you and any viewers or listeners to check out, called, 'Democratizing AI Compute.' It goes through the history of all the systems and the problems and challenges that they've run into, and it gets to, 'What is Modular doing about it?' So Part 11 talks about our architecture and the inside is Mojo, which is a programming language. I'll explain Mojo in a second. Next level out is called MAX. And so you can think of MAX as being a PyTorch replacement or a vLLM replacement, something that you can run on a single node and then get high performance LLM surveying, that kind of use case. And then the next level out is called Mammoth, and this is the cluster management Kubernetes layer. And so if you zoom in all the way back to Mojo, you say—your experience, you know what programming languages are, they're incredibly difficult and expensive to build.
paragraphs.zh: 让我放大讲讲我们的技术组成。我有一个博客文章系列，鼓励你和各位观众、听众去看，叫做「让 AI 计算民主化」。它回顾了所有系统的历史以及它们遭遇的问题和挑战，最终讲到「Modular 在做什么」。第 11 部分讲的是我们的架构，最里层是 Mojo，一门编程语言，我一会儿解释 Mojo。往外一层叫做 MAX，可以把 MAX 理解成 PyTorch 的替代品或者 vLLM 的替代品，一个可以在单节点上运行并获得高性能 LLM 服务的东西，那类使用场景。再往外一层叫做 Mammoth，这是集群管理的 Kubernetes 层。如果把焦距拉回到 Mojo，你作为有经验的人知道，编程语言是非常难构建、成本极高的东西。

paragraphs.en: Why would you do that in the first place? And the answer is, we had to. In fact, when we started Modular, I was like, 'I'm not going to invent a programming language.' I know that's a bad idea, it takes too long, it's too much work. You can't convince people to adopt a new language. I know all the reasons why creating language is actually a really bad idea. But it turns out, we were forced to do this because there is no good way to solve the problem. And the problem is, how do you write code that is portable across accelerators? So, that problem, I want portability across—for example, make it simple AMD and Nvidia GPUs, but then you layer on the fact that you're using a GPU because you want performance. And so I don't want a simplified, watered down—I want Java that runs on a GPU.
paragraphs.zh: 干嘛一开始就要这么做？答案是，我们不得不。事实上，当我们刚开始 Modular 的时候，我是说「我不打算发明一门编程语言。」我知道那是个坏主意，花的时间太长，工作量太大，没法说服人们采用一门新语言，我知道创建一门语言为什么其实是个非常坏的主意的所有原因。但结果发现，我们被逼着这么做，因为没有好的方法来解决这个问题。问题是，怎么写出能在各种加速器上可移植的代码？我想要可移植性——比如，简单来说就是 AMD 和 Nvidia GPU——但然后还要叠加上一个事实：你用 GPU 是因为你想要性能。所以我不想要一个简化的、稀释过的东西——我想要一个能在 GPU 上跑的 Java。

paragraphs.en: I want the full power of the GPU. I want to be able to deliver performance that meets and beats Nvidia on their own hardware. I want to have portability and unify this crazy compute where you have these really fancy heterogeneous systems and you have tensor cores and you have this explosion of complexity and innovation happening in this hardware platform layer. Most programming languages don't even know that there's an 8-bit floating point that exists. And so we looked around and I really did not want to have to do this, but it turns out that there really is no good answer. And again, we decided that, hey, the stakes are high, we want to do something impactful. We're willing to invest. I know what it takes to build a programming language. It's not rocket science, it's just a lot of really hard work and you need to set the team up to be incentivized the right way. But we decided that, yeah, let's do that.
paragraphs.zh: 我想要 GPU 的全部性能。我想能在 Nvidia 自己的硬件上交付与之媲美甚至超越的性能。我想要可移植性，统一这疯狂的计算——你有这些非常复杂的异构系统，有张量核心，硬件平台层正在发生的复杂性和创新爆炸式增长。大多数编程语言甚至不知道 8 位浮点数的存在。所以我们环顾四周，我真的不想不得不这么做，但结果发现真的没有好的答案。我们于是决定，好，赌注很高，我们想做有影响力的事，我们愿意投入。我知道构建一门编程语言需要什么，这不是火箭科学，只是大量非常艰难的工作，你需要把团队设置成有正确的激励。但我们决定，好，就这么干。

paragraphs.en: So I want to talk more about Mojo and its design, but before we do, maybe let's talk a little bit more about the pre-existing environment. I did actually read that blog post series. I recommended it to everyone. I think it's really great, and I want to talk a little bit about what the existing ecosystem of languages looks like, but even before then, can we talk more about the hardware? What does the space of hardware look like that people want to run these ML models on?
paragraphs.zh: 我想多聊聊 Mojo 及其设计，但在此之前，也许我们可以先多聊聊已有的环境。我确实读了那个博客系列，向所有人推荐过，我认为写得非常好。我想聊聊现有的语言生态系统是什么样子，但在那之前，我们能先多聊聊硬件吗？人们想要在哪些硬件上运行这些机器学习模型，这个硬件空间是什么样的？
